12   Which one of A–F correctly completes the following statement?

           Given that a < b, and f(x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b, the trapezium rule
                                        b
           produces an overestimate for a f(x) dx . . .

     A . . . if f  (x) > 0 and f  (x) < 0 for all x with a < x < b

     B . . . only if f  (x) > 0 and f  (x) < 0 for all x with a < x < b

     C . . . if and only if f  (x) > 0 and f  (x) < 0 for all x with a < x < b

     D . . . if f  (x) < 0 and f  (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b

     E . . . only if f  (x) < 0 and f  (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b

     F . . . if and only if f  (x) < 0 and f  (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b




                                                14

stem.rawText:
Which one of A–F correctly completes the following statement?

           Given that a < b, and f(x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b, the trapezium rule
                                        Bb
           produces an overestimate for a f(x) dx . . .

options.label: A
options.rawText: . . . if f  (x) > 0 and f  (x) < 0 for all x with a < x < b

options.label: B
options.rawText: . . . only if f  (x) > 0 and f  (x) < 0 for all x with a < x < b

options.label: C
options.rawText: . . . if and only if f  (x) > 0 and f  (x) < 0 for all x with a < x < b

options.label: D
options.rawText: . . . if f  (x) < 0 and f  (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b

options.label: E
options.rawText: . . . only if f  (x) < 0 and f  (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b

options.label: F
options.rawText: . . . if and only if f  (x) < 0 and f  (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b

solution.rawText:
The trapezium rule can produce an overestimate even if the function’s gradient goes from neg-
ative to positive, for example with the function f(x) = x2 + 1 between x = −1 and x = 1:

                                                   y




                                                                      x
                                 −1            0                1


Therefore neither the condition f 0 (x) > 0 for all x with a < x < b nor the condition f 0 (x) < 0
for all x with a < x < b is necessary for the trapezium rule to produce an overestimate. This
rejects the ‘only if’ options (B, C, E, F), leaving us with only A and D.
The above quadratic can also be used to help distinguish between these two options. We have
f 0 (x) = 2x and f 00 (x) = 2. Thus for −1 < x < 0, f 0 (x) < 0 and f 00 (x) > 0 and the trapezium rule
(with one strip) gives an overestimate. So D appears plausible.
On the other hand, we could reflect the quadratic to change the sign of f 00 (x): consider the
function f(x) = 1 − x2 .

                                                   y




                                                                      x
                                 −1            0                1


We now have f 0 (x) = −2x and f 00 (x) = −2. So when −1 < x < 0, we have f 0 (x) > 0 and
f 00 (x) < 0, yet the trapezium rule (with one strip) produces an underestimate. This shows that
A cannot be correct.
By elimination, the correct option must be D.
It is also possible to justify this: a function with f 00 (x) > 0 is ‘bending upwards’ (the technical
term is convex ). Any time we draw a chord between two points on the graph, the chord will lie
above the graph (except at the endpoints), so the trapezium rule estimate will be an overestimate
of the integral. In fact, this argument makes no assumptions about the sign of either f(x) or of
f 0 (x); having f 00 (x) > 0 for a < x < b is sufficient for the trapezium rule to give an overestimate
(but it is not necessary).
